title: A Guide to Accrued Expenses

interlinking_suggestions.title: Hiring Your First Subcontractor: Legal and Financial Steps
How to Manage Bookkeeping for Your Freelance Business
How to Read a Balance Sheet

author.name: Marcus Thorne
author.bio: A former tech COO turned 'Business-of-One' consultant, Marcus is obsessed with efficiency. He writes about optimizing workflows, leveraging technology, and building resilient systems for solo entrepreneurs.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: To eliminate year-end tax panic, calculate and transfer a percentage of every payment you receive into a dedicated tax savings account.
Determine your true monthly profit by subtracting the estimated costs of unbilled contractor work and usage-based software from the revenue they helped generate.
At the end of each month, physically transfer cash equal to all your accrued expenses into a separate high-yield savings account to turn theoretical liabilities into funded provisions.
You must manage your business with an accrual mindset for accurate decision-making, even if you file your taxes on a cash basis.
summary: Many solo professionals suffer from financial anxiety by relying on their bank balance, which ignores hidden liabilities like future tax bills and unpaid contractor fees, creating a false sense of profitability. The core advice is to adopt an accrual mindset by systematically estimating these expenses each month and physically setting aside the cash in a separate account. This simple discipline eliminates financial surprises, reveals your true net income, and empowers you to move from a reactive bookkeeper to a proactive CEO making decisions with clarity and confidence.
ai_overview_answer:
Tracking accrued expenses is the best defense against financial anxiety because it provides a real-time, accurate picture of your business's true profitability. This accounting shift involves recognizing costs you have incurred but have not yet been billed for, such as subcontractor fees, usage-based software, and estimated taxes. By matching these expenses to the revenue they helped generate in the same period, you see your actual net income, not just an inflated bank balance. This practice eliminates year-end tax panic by transforming your largest liability into a series of manageable, funded monthly amounts.

The most critical accrued expenses for solo professionals are payments to contractors, variable software costs, and income tax obligations. A simple framework involves identifying these liabilities monthly, creating a conservative estimate, and physically transferring that cash into a dedicated savings account. This discipline turns theoretical liabilities into fully-funded provisions. Ultimately, this proactive approach allows you to manage cash flow strategically and make decisions with confidence.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

seo.meta_title: Accrued Expenses Guide: Master Your Business Finances
seo.meta_description: This simple accrued expenses guide helps solo business owners eliminate tax panic and see their real profit. Stop reacting and start commanding your finances today.

@graph.headline: A Guide to Accrued Expenses
@graph.description: This simple accrued expenses guide helps solo business owners eliminate tax panic and see their real profit. Stop reacting and start commanding your finances today.
@graph.name: A 3-Step Framework for Turning Liabilities into a Strategic Tool

step.name: Identify Your Hidden Liabilities Monthly
step.text: Set aside 10 minutes at the end of each month for a "liability scan." Ask yourself one focused question: "Who have I received value from this month that I haven't paid yet?" You are not looking for invoices; you are looking for obligations. Make a simple list: your VA, your subcontractors, your usage-based software, and the government.

step.name: Quantify with a Conservative Estimate
step.text: You don't need perfection; you need prudence. For each liability, assign a realistic dollar value. For your developer, multiply their hours by their rate. For cloud services, check your usage dashboard and project the month-end total. For taxes, use the formula above. The golden rule is to always be conservative. It is far better to overestimate an expense by 10% and have a small cash surplus than to underestimate and face a shortfall.

step.name: Provision by Segregating the Cash
step.text: An estimate on a spreadsheet is meaningless without action. This final step makes your planning real. Open a separate, high-yield savings account named "Tax & Ops Savings." After you quantify your total accrued expenses for the month, physically transfer that exact amount of cash from your primary checking account into this dedicated account. This is the moment a theoretical liability becomes a fully-funded provision. The money is safe, untouchable, and ready the instant the invoice or tax deadline arrives.

mainEntity.name: How do freelancers calculate accrued taxes?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most reliable method is to set aside a percentage of every payment you receive. A common best practice is to estimate your total effective tax rate (federal, state, and self-employment) and apply it consistently. For a simple monthly calculation: (Monthly Gross Revenue) * (Your Estimated Blended Tax Rate %) = This Month's Accrued Tax. Then, transfer that amount to a dedicated savings account.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between accrued expenses and accounts payable for a consultant?

acceptedAnswer.text: The distinction hinges on the invoice. An accrued expense is an obligation for a service you've received before the bill arrives. Accounts payable is the formal liability created after you receive the bill.

mainEntity.name: What are the most common accrued expenses for a creative or technical professional?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Beyond taxes, your most common accrued expenses relate to the tools and talent you use to deliver your work.
- Subcontractor & Freelancer Fees: Payments to designers, developers, writers, or VAs who have completed work but not yet invoiced.
- Usage-Based Cloud & API Costs: Services like AWS, Google Cloud, or OpenAI where you consume resources throughout the month and get one bill at the end.
- Pro-rated Annual Software Subscriptions: A portion of an annual license fee technically "accrues" each month as you use it.
- Professional Services: Fees for legal or accounting advice that has been rendered but not yet billed.

mainEntity.name: Does tracking accrued expenses matter if I use cash-basis accounting for my taxes?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, absolutely. This is a critical distinction between managing your business and filing your taxes. While you may file taxes on a cash basis (recognizing income and expenses only when money changes hands), you must operate your business with an accrual mindset. This gives you a real-time, accurate picture of your true profitability, preventing you from making decisions based on incomplete data.

mainEntity.name: How do I record an accrued expense in my bookkeeping software?

acceptedAnswer.text:
In most software, you'll make a simple journal entry at the end of the month. The entry does two things:
1. It Debits (increases) an expense account (e.g., "Contractor Fees").
2. It Credits (increases) a liability account (e.g., "Accrued Expenses").
This ensures the expense is matched to the correct month, giving you an accurate profitability picture.

mainEntity.name: What happens to an accrued expense after the invoice is paid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Once the invoice arrives and you pay it, the temporary entry is reversed and closed out. At the start of the next month, you make a reversing journal entry that zeroes out the accrued liability. When you pay the actual invoice, you record it as a standard bill payment. This two-step process prevents double-counting the expense and keeps your books clean.

body.content: Why a Simple Accounting Shift is Your Best Defense Against Financial Anxiety

CEO-level confidence begins not with a complex spreadsheet, but with a fundamental change in how you view the money you owe. Understanding accrued expenses isn’t about appeasing an auditor; it’s about seizing control of your financial future. Mismanaging these hidden liabilities isn't a minor bookkeeping error—it's a catastrophic business risk. For the high-performing Business-of-One, mastering this concept is non-negotiable.
This mastery creates three immediate, powerful shifts in how you operate:

items: **It Eliminates Year-End Tax Panic.** The single biggest financial shock for most solo professionals is their tax bill. It feels unpredictable, arriving like a tidal wave that forces a scramble for cash. By systematically accruing your estimated tax liability each month—setting aside a portion of your income as you earn it—you dismantle that massive bill into a series of manageable payments. This simple discipline transforms your largest source of anxiety into a calm, orderly line item, ensuring the funds are ready long before the filing deadline.

**It Reveals Your True Profitability.** Relying on your bank balance to gauge business health is a trap. It’s a vanity metric that lies by omission. Imagine you billed $12,000 this month; the number in your checking account looks fantastic. But you owe your subcontractor $3,500 for work they completed and have incurred another $500 in usage-based software fees. Without accounting for those accrued expenses, you might make decisions based on a perceived $12,000 profit when your actual profit is only $8,000. Accrual accounting matches expenses to the revenue they helped generate in the same period, giving you a real-time, accurate picture of your net income.

**It Moves You from Reactive Bookkeeper to Proactive CEO.** This practice fundamentally shifts your mindset from backward-looking to forward-looking. A bookkeeper looks at last month's receipts to see what was spent. A CEO looks at the obligations forming *this month* to predict the cash needed for *next month*. This foresight is the foundation of strategic cash flow management. When you accurately track your accrued liabilities, you operate from a position of undeniable financial strength. You can confidently plan investments, take calculated risks, and decide when to hire, all because you have a true, uninflated view of your resources. You stop reacting to your business's finances and start commanding them.

body.content: Accrued Expenses vs. Accounts Payable: A Litmus Test for the Business-of-One

Commanding your finances begins with precision. While corporate accountants debate the finer points, the difference between an accrued expense and accounts payable is refreshingly simple for a solo professional. It all comes down to a single question:
***"Do I have an invoice in my hand?"***

That’s the test. An **accrued expense** is a cost your business has incurred for goods or services you've already benefited from, but for which you have not yet received a bill. The moment that invoice arrives, the obligation transforms. It becomes **accounts payable**—a formal, documented liability with a specific amount and due date.

rows: You've received the service or goods.
Often an estimate based on a contract or prior costs.
A precise, confirmed amount from the invoice.
An acknowledged but undocumented future payment.

body.content: Think about the virtual assistant (VA) who supports your business. They work for you all through March, delivering value every day. For that entire month, their fee is an **accrued expense**. You know you owe them the money, and you've benefited from their labor, but there's no official bill yet. On April 2nd, they email their invoice. Instantly, that debt is reclassified on your books from an accrued expense to accounts payable.

This distinction is the engine behind the **matching principle**, a core concept of accrual accounting. The principle dictates that you must report expenses in the same period as the revenue they helped generate. If you earned $10,000 in March thanks to your VA's help, you *must* account for their fee as a March expense to see your true March profit—even if you don't pay the bill until April. Failing to do this gives you a dangerously inflated sense of success, leading you to make strategic errors based on a reality that isn't real.
The 3 Hidden Liabilities That Can Sink a Solo Business

Financial truth isn't found in textbook examples of factory utility bills. Your reality is more dynamic, and the real risks to your cash flow are specific to how an elite professional operates. To build a resilient business, you must master the accrued expenses that competitors ignore. These are the three you must track without fail.

items:
**Liability #1: Your Support Network (Contractors & VAs)**
Your business thrives on a flexible network of talent—subcontractors, virtual assistants, and designers. This is a strategic advantage, but it comes with a hidden financial risk. Let’s say your developer wraps up a project on March 28th, but they invoice on the first of the month. If you only look at your bank account, March looks incredibly profitable. But that success is a mirage. You must accrue their estimated fee in the month the work was performed. This simple act ensures the expense is matched to the revenue it generated, giving you a true picture of your performance.

**Liability #2: Usage-Based Software & API Costs**
Your tech stack isn't just a collection of flat-fee subscriptions. You rely on powerful tools where cost is tied to consumption—think Amazon Web Services (AWS) for hosting, OpenAI for API calls, or email platforms that charge per subscriber. Each API call or gigabyte of data stored incrementally increases a liability that only becomes visible when the bill arrives. A project requiring heavy data processing could cause costs to spike unexpectedly. Proactively estimating and accruing these expenses is a crucial defense. Review your usage dashboards weekly to anticipate the month-end total, transforming a potential shock into a predictable operational expense.

**Liability #3: The Big One—Your Income Tax**
This is the largest and most anxiety-inducing liability for nearly every self-employed professional. You have no employer withholding taxes on your behalf; the responsibility is entirely yours. Treating your tax obligation as a future problem is a critical error. It is a real, growing expense you incur with every dollar of revenue you earn. Accruing for it monthly is the bedrock of a stable solo business.

body.content: Use this actionable formula at the end of each month:
`(Total Revenue To-Date * Your Estimated Blended Tax Rate %) - Taxes Already Paid This Year = Your Current Accrued Tax Liability`

Your "Blended Tax Rate" combines federal, state, and self-employment taxes. A conservative starting point for many professionals is 25-30% of gross income, but you should adjust this based on your income level and local tax laws. This consistent discipline transforms your largest financial fear into a manageable, well-funded line item.
A 3-Step Framework for Turning Liabilities into a Strategic Tool

Calculating these liabilities is the first step, but true financial control comes from applying a consistent system. This isn't defensive bookkeeping; it's a proactive framework for building a financial shield around your business, turning abstract numbers into tangible assets you can rely on.

items:
**Step 1: Identify Your Hidden Liabilities Monthly**
Set aside 10 minutes at the end of each month for a "liability scan." Ask yourself one focused question: *"Who have I received value from this month that I haven't paid yet?"* You are not looking for invoices; you are looking for obligations. Make a simple list: your VA, your subcontractors, your usage-based software, and the government.

**Step 2: Quantify with a Conservative Estimate**
You don't need perfection; you need prudence. For each liability, assign a realistic dollar value. For your developer, multiply their hours by their rate. For cloud services, check your usage dashboard and project the month-end total. For taxes, use the formula above. The golden rule is to **always be conservative**. It is far better to overestimate an expense by 10% and have a small cash surplus than to underestimate and face a shortfall.

**Step 3: Provision by Segregating the Cash**
An estimate on a spreadsheet is meaningless without action. This final step makes your planning real. Open a separate, high-yield savings account named "Tax & Ops Savings." After you quantify your total accrued expenses for the month, *physically transfer that exact amount of cash* from your primary checking account into this dedicated account. This is the moment a theoretical liability becomes a fully-funded provision. The money is safe, untouchable, and ready the instant the invoice or tax deadline arrives.

body.content: This discipline is the dividing line between amateur and professional finances. As self-employed accountant Eric Nisall states, "The biggest mistake freelancers make is not setting aside money for taxes from the very beginning... You are a business owner and need to plan for a tax liability." Physically separating this cash is your ultimate protection.
Frequently Asked Questions
Putting this system into practice is your shield, but specific questions often arise when theory meets reality. Here are clarifications for the most common points of confusion.

items:
**How do freelancers calculate accrued taxes?**
The most reliable method is to set aside a percentage of every payment you receive. A common best practice is to estimate your total effective tax rate (federal, state, and self-employment) and apply it consistently. For a simple monthly calculation: `(Monthly Gross Revenue) * (Your Estimated Blended Tax Rate %) = This Month's Accrued Tax`. Then, transfer that amount to a dedicated savings account.

**What is the difference between accrued expenses and accounts payable for a consultant?**
The distinction hinges on **the invoice**. An accrued expense is an obligation for a service you've received *before* the bill arrives. Accounts payable is the formal liability created *after* you receive the bill.

rows: Your developer works 20 hours for you in March, but they won't invoice until April. It's March 31st.
You must estimate and record this to see your true March profitability, even without an invoice.
On April 5th, your developer sends the invoice for their March work.
The estimate becomes a formal, fixed amount owed to a specific vendor.

items:
**What are the most common accrued expenses for a creative or technical professional?**
Beyond taxes, your most common accrued expenses relate to the tools and talent you use to deliver your work.
*   **Subcontractor & Freelancer Fees:** Payments to designers, developers, writers, or VAs who have completed work but not yet invoiced.
*   **Usage-Based Cloud & API Costs:** Services like AWS, Google Cloud, or OpenAI where you consume resources throughout the month and get one bill at the end.
*   **Pro-rated Annual Software Subscriptions:** A portion of an annual license fee technically "accrues" each month as you use it.
*   **Professional Services:** Fees for legal or accounting advice that has been rendered but not yet billed.

**Does tracking accrued expenses matter if I use cash-basis accounting for my taxes?**
**Yes, absolutely.** This is a critical distinction between managing your business and filing your taxes. While you may file taxes on a cash basis (recognizing income and expenses only when money changes hands), you must *operate* your business with an accrual mindset. This gives you a real-time, accurate picture of your true profitability, preventing you from making decisions based on incomplete data.

**How do I record an accrued expense in my bookkeeping software?**
In most software, you'll make a simple journal entry at the end of the month. The entry does two things:
1.  It **Debits** (increases) an expense account (e.g., "Contractor Fees").
2.  It **Credits** (increases) a liability account (e.g., "Accrued Expenses").
This ensures the expense is matched to the correct month, giving you an accurate profitability picture.

**What happens to an accrued expense after the invoice is paid?**
Once the invoice arrives and you pay it, the temporary entry is reversed and closed out. At the start of the next month, you make a reversing journal entry that zeroes out the accrued liability. When you pay the actual invoice, you record it as a standard bill payment. This two-step process prevents double-counting the expense and keeps your books clean.

body.content: From Bookkeeping Chore to CEO Command Center

This system is about more than tidy bookkeeping—it's about fundamentally upgrading how you lead. This practice is the bridge between being a reactive owner managed by daily tasks and becoming a forward-looking CEO who commands their financial destiny.

You cannot control what you cannot see. Unbilled contractor fees, looming tax payments, and climbing software costs create a constant, low-level financial fog. Relying on your bank balance is like flying a plane through clouds with no instruments—you feel like you're moving forward, but you have no real idea of your actual position. The discipline of accrual accounting turns on the instruments. It makes invisible obligations visible, replacing guesswork with a clear, accurate picture of your business's true financial health.
This clarity is the foundation of control. With it, your decision-making transforms.

items: **Strategic Spending:** Can you really afford that new equipment? A glance at your balance sheet, complete with accrued liabilities, gives you the real answer.
**Confident Pricing:** Knowing your true project expenses—including all accrued costs—allows you to price your services for sustainable profit, not just immediate cash.

**Smarter Growth:** This foresight allows you to move from a reactive state to proactively shaping your financial future, building a resilient business that can weather shocks and seize opportunities.

body.content: Ultimately, this is a shift in mindset. It's the act of taking radical ownership of every dollar your business owes, not just the ones with an invoice attached. This discipline dissolves financial anxiety because there are no more surprises hiding in the shadows. You have replaced uncertainty with data and fear with foresight. This is how you operate with the confidence of a true CEO.
